result:
Research complete. I pulled authoritative facts from five primary Adobe Experience League pages covering the GraphQL Content Fragments API, persisted queries, remote-query authentication, the React headless client, and access tokens.

Key takeaways for your AEMaaCS pure-headless React stack:

- **Delivery path**: Use `GET /graphql/execute.json/<CONFIG>/<QUERY_NAME>` (persisted queries) — it's the only CDN/Dispatcher-cacheable option. POST queries are not cacheable and are blocked on publish by default.
- **Auth reality check**: The Cloud Manager local dev token is **24h** and runs as the issuing user — fine for development, but production server-side calls need Service Credentials (IMS token exchange), not the dev token.
- **CORS** is the classic trap for a standalone external React app — AEMaaCS won't accept arbitrary origins without a deployed crossorigin OSGi config (or a backend proxy).
- **SDK idiom**: `@adobe/aem-headless-client-js` with `new AEMHeadless({ serviceURL, endpoint, auth })` + `runPersistedQuery()`, configured via `REACT_APP_*` env vars; develop/persist on author, promote to publish.

All structured fields (domain, key_apis, idioms, gotchas, version_notes, sources) have been submitted.

structured_output.domain: AEM as a Cloud Service (AEMaaCS) headless delivery of Content Fragments via the GraphQL API, consumed by a standalone external React SPA authenticated with a Cloud Manager local dev/service token.
key_apis: Execute persisted query: GET /graphql/execute.json/<CONFIG>/<QUERY_NAME> — the recommended, CDN/Dispatcher-cacheable delivery path on publish.
Create/persist a query: PUT /graphql/persist.json/<CONFIG>/<QUERY_NAME> with the raw GraphQL string (or JSON body with query + cache-control) — done on author, then promoted to publish.
Variables on persisted queries are appended to the URL after a semicolon: /graphql/execute.json/<CONFIG>/<QUERY>;var1=val1;var2=val2 (special chars MUST be URL-encoded: ; = %3B, = = %3D, / = %2F).
Direct ad-hoc query: POST /content/_cq_graphql/<CONFIG>/endpoint.json (or global endpoint) — useful in dev/GraphiQL, NOT cacheable, blocked by Dispatcher on publish by default.

@adobe/aem-headless-client-js: new AEMHeadless({ serviceURL, endpoint, auth }) where auth is a Bearer token string or [user, pass]; methods runPersistedQuery(path, params, options), persistQuery(), listPersistedQueries(), runQuery() (POST).
Query field shapes: single byPath (e.g. authorByPath(_path)), list (authorList { items {...} }), and cursor paginated (adventurePaginated(first, after){ edges { node {...} } pageInfo {...} }).

Filtering/sorting/pagination args: filter with _expressions/value/_operator (EQUALS, CONTAINS, STARTS_WITH) + _logOp (AND/OR); sort 'field ASC|DESC'; list uses offset/limit; *Paginated uses first/after (default 50, max 100).
System fields prefixed with underscore: _path, _metadata, _variation, _tags, _model, _locale, _dynamicUrl (web-optimized asset URL), _dmS7Url (Dynamic Media URL).
idioms: Author/promote workflow: develop & test queries in the GraphiQL IDE on AEM author, persist them there, then transfer/replicate the persisted query to publish; the React app only ever calls GET /graphql/execute.json.
Drive every delivery call through persisted queries (GET) so responses are cached by CDN + Dispatcher; reserve POST runQuery for development and GraphiQL.
Use the official @adobe/aem-headless-client-js SDK in the React app rather than hand-rolling fetch; wrap calls in a small data layer and read results off response.data.
Configure the SPA via REACT_APP_* env vars (REACT_APP_HOST_URI/serviceURL, REACT_APP_GRAPHQL_ENDPOINT, REACT_APP_AUTH/token, REACT_APP_PROJECT) loaded into a context.js helper.
Define a named GraphQL endpoint per Content Fragment Model configuration (or use the 'global' config); endpoint must be explicitly enabled/published for headless delivery.
Tune caching per persisted query via cache-control / surrogate-control in the PUT body (max-age, s-maxage, stale-while-revalidate, stale-if-error) instead of relying only on defaults.
Request only the variations and fields you need (use _variation and explicit field selection) to keep payloads lean.
gotchas: The Cloud Manager Local Development Token expires after 24 hours and carries the permissions of the user who generated it — fine for dev, but production server-side integrations must use Service Credentials / Technical Account (IMS JWT-or-OAuth -> access token), not the dev token.
All requests authenticated with a token run AS the generating user account, so that account must hold the GraphQL/Content Fragment read permissions or queries silently return empty/403.
POST GraphQL queries are not cacheable and the Dispatcher blocks them on publish by default — using them in production kills CDN caching and may 404/403.

CORS bites standalone external React apps: AEMaaCS does not allow arbitrary origins by default — you must deploy a CORS config (crossorigin OSGi policy) allowlisting the app origin, or proxy through your own backend.

Querying the AEM author environment from a browser app relies on an existing author session in another tab; author is read-only for GraphQL and not meant for production delivery — point delivery at publish.
Persisted-query URL variables use semicolon separators and require strict URL-encoding; forgetting to encode ; = / breaks the request.
Hard limits: query length 1,048,576 chars, 15,000 tokens, 200,000 whitespace tokens; paginated first caps at 100 — large result sets need pagination.
A Content Fragment Model change requires republishing the model/endpoint; the GraphQL schema is generated from enabled models, so missing or unpublished models produce schema gaps.
structured_output.version_notes: Scope is AEM as a Cloud Service (continuously released; no fixed version number — features ship via regular cloud updates). The GraphQL Content Fragment API and persisted queries are GA on AEMaaCS; equivalent functionality exists on AEM 6.5 but with feature lag (e.g., advanced cursor pagination, _dynamicUrl/_dmS7Url and some filter capabilities are AEMaaCS-first). Local Development Access Token is generated in the Cloud Manager Developer Console (Integrations tab) and is valid 24h max. Persisted-query default publish cache headers observed: max-age 60s, s-maxage 7200s, stale-while-revalidate 86400s, stale-if-error 86400s. Cursor pagination (*Paginated) defaults to first=50, max 100. The JS SDK is @adobe/aem-headless-client-js (also a Node variant). Facts current as of June 2026 per Adobe Experience League docs.

Persisted GraphQL queries — AEM as a Cloud Service
Authentication for Remote AEM GraphQL Queries on Content Fragments
AEM Headless APIs and React (aem-headless-client-js) — AEM Headless first tutorial
Local Development Access Token — Generating Access Tokens for Server-Side APIs
